title: Design Patterns Overview: How to Learn, Apply, and Combine Them
excerpt: A practical overview of design patterns: what they are, where they came from, when to use them, and how to build real mastery instead of memorizing names.

body.value:
A design pattern is a general, reusable solution to a recurring software design problem in a specific context.

It is not a finished library, framework, or copy-paste implementation. It is a proven structure for solving a kind of problem that appears again and again.

Patterns matter because they help developers move from accidental design to intentional design. Instead of inventing a brand-new approach every time, you can recognize the situation and apply a known solution.

The idea of patterns did not begin in software. It came from architecture and urban design, where Christopher Alexander described recurring solutions for towns, buildings, and spaces.

Software developers later adapted this idea to object-oriented design. The most influential milestone was the classic Gang of Four book, which organized software design patterns into categories and gave the industry a shared vocabulary.

That shared vocabulary is one of the biggest reasons patterns became so valuable. Teams could stop describing every solution from scratch and start talking at the level of intent.

value.caption: How the idea of patterns moved into software
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Architecture[City and building architecture] --> RecurringSolutions[Recurring solutions to recurring problems]
RecurringSolutions --> Software[Software design]
Software --> PatternCatalog[Named pattern catalogs]
PatternCatalog --> SharedLanguage[Shared design language for teams]

body.value:
Patterns help you avoid reinventing the wheel. Many design problems have already been explored, discussed, refined, and tested in real systems. Knowing this saves time and reduces accidental complexity.

Patterns also improve communication. Saying use a repository or this should be a strategy is much clearer than describing a long sequence of refactorings in a code review.

Most importantly, patterns help you produce software that is easier to change. They often reduce coupling, isolate variation, and make behavior easier to extend, test, and reason about.

value.caption: Why patterns improve development
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
RecurringProblem --> RecognizePattern
RecognizePattern --> ReuseKnownStructure
ReuseKnownStructure --> BetterCommunication
ReuseKnownStructure --> BetterDesign
BetterDesign --> EasierTesting
BetterDesign --> EasierChange
BetterCommunication --> FasterTeamAlignment

body.value:
One of the most practical benefits of patterns is communication. Without a pattern name, a developer might explain a solution as a series of low-level refactorings: extract an interface, move persistence logic, inject it into the class, and stop calling the database directly.

With pattern vocabulary, the same idea becomes much simpler: use the Repository pattern to decouple data access.

That shift is important. It compresses design discussion into a shared concept and lets the team focus on trade-offs instead of mechanics.

value.title: How to learn patterns the right way

body.value:
Learning patterns is not about memorizing a catalog. Real learning happens in stages.

First, there is ignorance: you do not know the pattern exists. Then comes awareness: you have heard of it and know it might help. After that comes experimentation: you try it, often too eagerly, and begin discovering where it fits and where it does not. Finally comes mastery: you can recognize opportunities quickly and apply the pattern with confidence and restraint.

The best goal is to become T-shaped. Know many patterns broadly enough to follow design conversations, but go deep on the ones that appear often in the systems you actually build.

value.caption: Practical learning path for design patterns
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Ignorance[Ignorance] --> Awareness[Awareness]
Awareness --> Experimentation[Experimentation]
Experimentation --> Mastery[Mastery]
Mastery --> Judgment[Good judgment about fit and trade-offs]

body.value:
A mature pattern is more than a name. It usually includes a standard set of information: its name, classification, intent, motivation, applicability, structure, participants, collaborations, consequences, implementation guidance, known uses, and related patterns.

If you only want broad awareness, focus first on three things: the name, the intent, and the situations where the pattern applies. That is enough to recognize when a pattern might help.

If you want mastery, you must go deeper. You should understand the structure, the participant roles, the collaboration between them, and the trade-offs the pattern introduces.

value.caption: What makes up a design pattern
value.diagram:
mindmap
  root((Design Pattern))
    Name
    Intent
    Motivation
    Applicability
    Structure
    Participants
    Collaboration
    Consequences
    Implementation
    Known Uses
    Related Patterns

value.title: How diagrams fit into pattern thinking

body.value:
Patterns are often taught with UML-style structure diagrams because patterns are fundamentally about relationships. A diagram helps you see abstractions, implementations, dependencies, and interactions at a glance.

But structure alone is not enough. Several patterns can look similar in diagrams while serving different purposes. Two patterns may share almost the same shape yet differ completely in intent.

That is why pattern learning should always connect structure with context. Do not just memorize diagrams. Learn what problem the structure is solving.

value.caption: The same abstraction can support multiple implementations
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Client --> IService
IService --> RealService
IService --> Proxy
Proxy --> RealService

body.value:
The worst time to apply a newly learned pattern is impulsively in production code. New pattern knowledge often leads to overuse. Everything starts to look like a nail.

A better approach is to practice in a controlled environment: coding katas, refactoring exercises, and small experiments backed by tests. That lets you understand the pattern without risking critical code.

When applying patterns to real code, use refactoring discipline. Start with test coverage, make changes in small steps, work in a separate branch, review the result, and confirm behavior is unchanged. If the pattern does not fit, delete the attempt and keep the lesson.

value.caption: Safe pattern adoption workflow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
LearnPattern --> PracticeInKata
PracticeInKata --> AddTests
AddTests --> RefactorInSmallSteps
RefactorInSmallSteps --> ReviewDesign
ReviewDesign --> KeepOrDiscard
KeepOrDiscard --> BetterJudgment

value.title: Patterns become more powerful when combined

body.value:
In real systems, patterns are rarely used alone. Strong designs often come from combining a few simple patterns.

For example, a Strategy can encapsulate one family of behavior, a Repository can isolate persistence, a Decorator can add caching or logging, and a Builder can create a configured object safely. Each pattern handles a different kind of responsibility.

This matters because patterns are not trophies. Their value is not in how many you know, but in how well you combine them to solve a real problem with minimal complexity.

value.caption: Pattern combinations in a realistic design
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Client --> Service
Service --> Strategy
Service --> Repository
Repository --> Decorator
Decorator --> DataStore
Builder --> Service

body.value: This example shows how a pattern name compresses a design decision into something concrete. The service depends on an abstraction instead of directly calling persistence logic. That is the core benefit behind a Repository-style design.

value.caption: A simple repository-based design in C#

body.value:
If you want a practical starting point, begin with patterns that appear often in application code.

  • Strategy helps when behavior varies and large conditionals start growing.
  • Repository helps isolate persistence concerns from business logic.
  • Adapter helps when two interfaces do not naturally fit together.
  • Factory helps when object creation is complex or should be hidden.
  • Proxy and Decorator help add control or extra behavior around an object.
  • Singleton is worth understanding, both for how it works and why it is often misused.

You do not need to master every pattern at once. Start with the ones that solve problems you see every week.

• Memorizing names without understanding intent
  • Applying a pattern before a real design problem exists
  • Choosing patterns because they look clever rather than because they fit
  • Ignoring trade-offs such as added indirection and complexity
  • Using production code as a first practice environment
  • Learning structures but not learning the context where they help
  • Treating one favorite pattern as a universal solution

value.title: Design patterns are design tools
value.text: Patterns help you recognize recurring problems, communicate intent clearly, and apply proven structures instead of inventing everything from scratch. Learn them broadly, practice them deliberately, and use them only when the problem truly matches.

title: C# Strategy Pattern Explained: Runtime Behavior Without Conditionals Everywhere
excerpt: Learn how the Strategy pattern works in C#, why it improves extensibility and testability, and how to apply it for tax calculation, invoice delivery, shipping providers, and existing .NET APIs like Array.Sort.

body.value:
The Strategy pattern is one of the most practical design patterns in everyday C# development. It helps when an application needs to perform the same kind of operation in multiple different ways, and the exact behavior should be selected at runtime.

Instead of placing all variations inside one growing class filled with if, switch, or nested conditionals, Strategy moves each algorithm into its own implementation behind a shared interface.

The result is a design where behavior becomes replaceable. The object using the behavior does not need to know the implementation details. It only needs to know the contract.

value.caption: Core idea of the Strategy pattern
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Context --> IStrategy
IStrategy --> StrategyA[ConcreteStrategyA]
IStrategy --> StrategyB[ConcreteStrategyB]
IStrategy --> StrategyC[ConcreteStrategyC]

body.value:
The Strategy pattern is usually described through three roles.

  • Context: the object that needs some behavior but should not own all implementation details.
  • Strategy: an interface that defines the operation the context can use.
  • Concrete Strategy: a specific implementation of that operation.

In business code, the context is often something like an order service, checkout workflow, pricing engine, or export processor. The strategy interface captures the behavior that varies, and the concrete strategies implement the actual rules.

value.title: A good rule of thumb
value.text: When the same business action can happen in several valid ways and the choice should be made at runtime, Strategy is often a strong candidate.

body.value: This is the minimal shape of a Strategy pattern in C#. The Order is the context. It delegates tax calculation to whatever implementation of ISalesTaxStrategy it receives.

value.caption: Minimal Strategy pattern in C#

value.title: Why this is better than a large conditional

body.value:
Without Strategy, the context often accumulates country checks, delivery type checks, provider checks, and API-specific logic. That produces code that is harder to maintain, harder to test, and more dangerous to change.

With Strategy, each variation lives in a focused class. Adding a new behavior usually means adding a new implementation instead of editing a giant conditional that already handles many unrelated cases.

value.caption: From conditional-heavy design to replaceable behavior
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Before[Large context class] --> IfElse[if/switch on country or option]
IfElse --> MixedLogic[Mixed rules and implementation details]

After[Context] --> StrategyInterface[IStrategy]
StrategyInterface --> FocusedA[Focused Strategy A]
StrategyInterface --> FocusedB[Focused Strategy B]
StrategyInterface --> FocusedC[Focused Strategy C]

value.title: Sales tax example: a strong real-world fit

body.value:
A tax calculator is a natural example for Strategy because tax rules vary by country, region, and sometimes item type. The context should not need to know the implementation details for every possible region.

One strategy can calculate Swedish tax rules, another can calculate United States tax rules, and both remain isolated from each other. This keeps the design open for extension without forcing the central order logic to keep growing.

value.caption: A more realistic Swedish tax strategy

body.value:
This is where Strategy becomes especially valuable. You can improve one country-specific implementation without risking accidental changes to the others.

That isolation also makes tests much more focused. A change to Swedish tax rules should not break tests for the United States tax implementation.

value.title: Runtime selection is the key benefit

body.value:
The real power of Strategy is not just extracting classes. It is selecting behavior at runtime based on user input, configuration, or business rules.

For example, if the destination country is Sweden, the application can choose SwedenSalesTaxStrategy. If the destination is the United States, it can choose UnitedStatesSalesTaxStrategy. The context stays unchanged.

value.caption: Runtime decision flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
UserInput[User input / order data] --> Resolver[Strategy selection]
Resolver --> Sweden[SwedenSalesTaxStrategy]
Resolver --> USA[UnitedStatesSalesTaxStrategy]
Resolver --> Other[Other region strategy]
Sweden --> Context
USA --> Context
Other --> Context

value.title: An alternative approach: pass the strategy into the method

body.value:
The strategy does not have to be stored as a property on the context. Another valid approach is to pass the strategy directly into the method that needs it.

This is useful when the behavior is more temporary, when you want a one-off override, or when you want to keep the context itself lighter.

value.caption: Passing the strategy directly to the method

body.value: This is still Strategy. The important idea is not where the interface is stored. The important idea is that behavior is represented as an interchangeable implementation behind a contract.

value.title: Invoice delivery is another excellent Strategy example

body.value:
Invoice generation often needs multiple delivery mechanisms: email, file export, print-on-demand, database storage, or perhaps an external billing API.

These are clearly different implementations of the same business capability: generate or deliver an invoice. That makes them a strong fit for Strategy.

value.caption: Invoice delivery strategies
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Order --> IInvoiceStrategy
IInvoiceStrategy --> Email[EmailInvoiceStrategy]
IInvoiceStrategy --> File[FileInvoiceStrategy]
IInvoiceStrategy --> Print[PrintOnDemandInvoiceStrategy]

value.caption: Invoice Strategy with shared base functionality

value.title: Shipping providers show Strategy beyond pure computation

body.value:
Strategy is not limited to calculations. It also works when different providers, services, or APIs must be called in different ways.

A shipping workflow is a perfect example. DHL, FedEx, UPS, USPS, and local postal services may all require different request shapes, validation rules, authentication, and response handling. The business intent is the same, but the algorithm and integration details vary.

value.caption: Shipping providers as strategies

body.value: This keeps each provider-specific integration local to one class. The order or order service simply asks for shipping to happen. It does not need to know how a specific provider works.

value.title: The context can be a service, not only a domain entity

body.value:
In small examples, the context is often modeled as the domain object itself, such as Order. In larger applications, it is often cleaner for the context to be a service layer object like OrderService, CheckoutService, or InvoiceService.

That approach keeps the domain model focused on business state while orchestration and dependency coordination live in the service layer.

value.caption: Service-oriented version of the Strategy pattern
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Controller --> OrderService
OrderService --> TaxStrategy
OrderService --> InvoiceStrategy
OrderService --> ShippingStrategy
OrderService --> Order

body.value:
One of the strongest practical benefits of Strategy is testability.

Each concrete strategy can be tested independently with focused input. At the same time, the context can be tested using mocked or fake strategies to verify orchestration without executing real email, filesystem, tax, or HTTP logic.

value.caption: Testing a context with a fake strategy

value.title: Why testability improves
value.text: The context no longer owns the implementation details. That lets you test the context and each algorithm separately instead of trying to verify one giant class that does everything.

body.value:
The Strategy pattern appears all over .NET, often without explicitly using the word Strategy. A good example is Array.Sort with IComparer<T>.

When you pass a comparer, you are effectively injecting the strategy for how two elements should be compared. The sorting algorithm stays the same, but the comparison behavior can change.

value.caption: Array.Sort with IComparer<T> as a strategy

body.value: This is an important lesson: if a method accepts an interface or abstraction that changes behavior, there is a good chance Strategy is already in play.

• Extensibility: new behaviors can be added by creating new strategies.
  • Cleaner separation of concerns: the context coordinates work, while each strategy owns one algorithm.
  • Reduced conditional complexity: large branching logic is replaced with focused implementations.
  • Testability: strategies and contexts can be tested independently.
  • Runtime flexibility: behavior can be selected based on user input or configuration.

Like every pattern, Strategy improves some things and costs some things.

  • It introduces more classes and abstractions.
  • It can be overkill if there are only one or two trivial variations.
  • Bad naming can make the design harder to understand.
  • If strategy selection logic becomes messy, the creation side may need its own refactoring, often with a factory or resolver.

Use it where variability is real and likely to grow, not just because an interface can be introduced.

value.title: A common mistake
value.text: Do not apply Strategy only to look architecturally sophisticated. Apply it when behavior truly varies and you need to swap algorithms cleanly.

value.title: When Strategy is a strong fit

body.value:
• You have multiple ways to perform the same operation.
  • The choice should happen at runtime.
  • Conditionals are starting to spread through the codebase.
  • Different rules should evolve independently.
  • You want to isolate integrations with different external providers.
  • You want easier unit testing with mocks, stubs, or fake implementations.

value.title: Strategy in one sentence
value.text: Define a family of interchangeable behaviors behind a shared interface, then choose the appropriate implementation at runtime without changing the context.

title: Command Pattern in C#: Decoupling Actions and Enabling Undo/Redo
excerpt: Learn how the Command Pattern works in C#, how to implement it, and why it enables clean architecture, undo/redo functionality, and extensibility in modern applications.

body.value:
The Command Pattern is a behavioral design pattern that turns a request into an object. This allows you to parameterize clients with different requests, queue or log operations, and support undo/redo functionality.

Instead of calling methods directly, you wrap the action inside a command object. This decouples the sender of a request from the receiver.

value.caption: Command Pattern Structure
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Client --> Invoker
Invoker --> Command
Command --> Receiver

body.value:
The Command Pattern consists of four key elements:

  • Command – Encapsulates the request and required data
  • Receiver – Performs the actual work
  • Invoker – Executes the command
  • Client – Creates and configures the command

value.caption: Real-world Example: Shopping Cart
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Button(Client) --> Manager(Invoker)
Manager --> AddToCartCommand(Command)
AddToCartCommand --> ShoppingCart(Receiver)

body.value:
The Command Pattern is powerful because it enables:

  • Decoupling between UI and business logic
  • Undo/Redo functionality
  • Command history tracking
  • Delayed execution
  • Replaying operations

value.text: Commands can be stored and replayed later, making this pattern ideal for event sourcing and recovery scenarios.

body.value: This interface defines the contract for all commands. Each command must know how to execute itself and optionally undo its action.
The CommandManager acts as the invoker. It executes commands and keeps track of them using a stack, enabling undo operations in reverse order.

value.caption: Undo Mechanism (LIFO)
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Cmd1 --> Cmd2 --> Cmd3
Undo --> Cmd3 --> Cmd2 --> Cmd1

body.value: This command contains everything it needs to execute: references to repositories and the product itself. It also defines how to undo the action.
Instead of calling repositories directly, the application now works through commands:

value.code:
var command = new AddToCartCommand(cartRepo, productRepo, product);
manager.Invoke(command);

body.value: This removes tight coupling between the UI and the data layer.
In WPF, the Command Pattern is built into the framework via ICommand. UI elements like buttons bind directly to commands.
The RelayCommand bridges UI and business logic, allowing reuse of your core command implementations.

Benefits:

  • Clean separation of concerns
  • Improved testability
  • Flexible and extensible design
  • Undo/Redo support

Tradeoffs:

  • Additional abstraction layer
  • More classes and complexity
  • Overkill for small applications

value.text: Use the Command Pattern in complex applications where flexibility, extensibility, and undo functionality are important.

body.value:
The Command Pattern encapsulates actions as objects, enabling powerful features like undo/redo, decoupling, and extensibility. It is widely used in UI frameworks like WPF and in complex business applications.

When applied correctly, it leads to cleaner, more maintainable architecture — especially in systems with evolving requirements.

title: Eliminating Null Checks in C# with the Null Object Pattern
excerpt: Learn how to eliminate repetitive null checks in C# using the Null Object pattern. Reduce cyclomatic complexity, improve readability, and make your code safer with default object implementations.

body.value:
Null values are deeply embedded in modern programming languages, but they come with a cost. Introduced in 1965 by Tony Hoare, the null reference was intended as a convenience—but later described by its creator as a "billion-dollar mistake".

The problem is simple: null introduces uncertainty. Every time we interact with an object, we must ask:

  • Is this object null?
  • Are its properties null?
  • Will this cause a runtime exception?

This leads to defensive programming and repeated null checks scattered throughout the codebase.

value.caption: Typical flow when dealing with nullable objects
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Receive Object] --> B{Is Null?}
B -->|Yes| C[Handle Null]
B -->|No| D[Use Object]
D --> E{Property Null?}
E -->|Yes| F[Handle Again]
E -->|No| G[Continue]

value.title: Why Null Checks Hurt Your Code

body.value:
Null checks may seem harmless, but they introduce several problems:

  • Increased cyclomatic complexity – every null check adds branching logic
  • Reduced readability – business logic gets buried under defensive code
  • Error-prone code – missing a check leads to runtime exceptions

Even simple constructors or render methods quickly become cluttered with validation logic.

value.caption: Typical null-check-heavy code

body.value:
The Null Object Pattern replaces null values with a default object that implements the same interface. Instead of checking for null, your code always interacts with a valid object.

This object provides safe, default behavior and eliminates the need for defensive checks.

value.caption: Null Object replaces null values with a safe default implementation
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Request Object] --> B{Exists?}
B -->|Yes| C[Real Object]
B -->|No| D[Null Object]
C --> E[Use Normally]
D --> E

body.value: The pattern is typically implemented using an interface shared by both the real object and the null object.

value.caption: Shared interface
Concrete implementation
Null Object implementation

body.value: Instead of returning null from a service or repository, return a Null Object. This guarantees that consumers always receive a valid instance.

value.caption: Returning a Null Object instead of null
Consumer code without null checks

value.title: Benefits of the Null Object Pattern

body.value:
• No null checks – cleaner and more readable code
  • Reduced cyclomatic complexity
  • Safer execution – no NullReferenceExceptions
  • Better design – behavior is encapsulated in objects

Use the Null Object Pattern when:

  • You frequently check for null values
  • You want to simplify conditional logic
  • A default "do nothing" behavior makes sense

Avoid it when null represents a truly exceptional or invalid state that should not be silently handled.

value.title: Key Insight
value.text: The Null Object Pattern doesn't remove null—it replaces it with behavior. Instead of checking for absence, you design for safe defaults.

title: C# Specification Pattern Explained: Reusing Domain Rules for Validation and Queries
excerpt: Learn how the Specification Pattern works in C#, why strongly typed specifications matter, how to combine them, and how to use them for in-memory validation and efficient database queries.

body.value:
The Specification Pattern is a design pattern used to encapsulate a single piece of domain knowledge into a reusable object. In practice, it becomes valuable when the same business rule must be applied in more than one place.

A common example is a rule like movie is suitable for children. That rule may be needed when validating a user action in memory, and also when querying the database for matching records. Without a shared abstraction, teams often duplicate the same business logic in repositories, services, handlers, and UI-facing code.

The pattern helps solve two problems at once: duplication of domain rules and loss of maintainability. Instead of scattering business conditions across the codebase, you place them in dedicated specifications and reuse them consistently.

value.caption: The core idea behind Specification
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
BusinessRule[Business rule] --> Specification[Specification]
Specification --> Validation[In-memory validation]
Specification --> Querying[Database querying]
Specification --> Creation[Creation of matching objects]

body.value:
The pattern is most often discussed in the context of three use cases.

  • In-memory validation — check whether an already loaded object satisfies some business rule.
  • Database querying — find records that match the same rule efficiently at the SQL level.
  • Creation of new objects — describe what a valid candidate should look like and search for or generate examples that satisfy the rule.

In day-to-day business applications, the first two are usually the most important. They are also where duplication appears most often.

value.title: The practical trigger
value.text: The Specification Pattern starts paying off when the same business condition must be used in at least two places, especially validation and database filtering.

body.value:
A good teaching example is a movie catalog. Imagine a Movie entity with fields like name, release date, MPAA rating, genre, and regular rating.

Now imagine two business rules:

  • A movie is suitable for children when its MPAA rating is G or PG.
  • A movie has a CD or DVD version when it was released more than six months ago.

Those rules can influence both searching and purchasing. You may want to filter movies for kids in the database, and also validate that a child ticket cannot be bought for a movie rated R or PG-13.

value.caption: Where the same rule appears more than once
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Rule1[Movie suitable for children] --> Search1[Search only kid-friendly movies]
Rule1 --> Validation1[Validate child ticket purchase]
Rule2[Movie available on CD/DVD] --> Search2[Search movies available on disc]
Rule2 --> Validation2[Validate CD purchase]

body.value:
Without specifications, developers usually end up writing the same rule more than once. One version appears in the repository as a LINQ Where clause, and another version appears in service or UI logic as an if statement.

That seems harmless at first, but over time it increases technical debt. When a rule changes, every copy must be found and updated. Missing even one occurrence creates inconsistent behavior.

The natural instinct is to move the rule into a method on the entity, such as movie.IsSuitableForChildren(). That improves reuse for in-memory validation, but causes a different problem when used inside ORM queries.

value.caption: Typical duplication without Specification

value.title: Why entity methods do not solve querying

body.value:
Most ORMs can translate simple expression trees into SQL, but they usually cannot translate arbitrary custom methods. That means code like .Where(x => x.IsSuitableForChildren()) often fails at runtime.

The ORM can understand comparisons like x.MpaaRating <= MpaaRating.PG, but not your custom domain method unless it has a known SQL equivalent. The fallback is to fetch all rows first and filter in memory, but that is usually inefficient and scales poorly.

This creates an uncomfortable choice: either duplicate the rule or use an inefficient query. The Specification Pattern is designed to avoid that trade-off.

value.caption: The trade-off without Specification
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Duplicate rule] --> B[Works in query]
A --> C[Works in validation]
A --> D[Technical debt]
E[Move rule into entity method] --> F[Great encapsulation]
E --> G[ORM cannot translate method]
G --> H[Fetch everything first]
H --> I[Performance cost]

body.value:
To understand Specification in C#, it helps to understand the split between IEnumerable<T> and IQueryable<T>.

  • IEnumerable works with in-memory objects and uses delegates.
  • IQueryable works with expression trees that providers such as Entity Framework or NHibernate inspect and translate to SQL.

This distinction matters because a lambda in C# can become either a delegate or an expression tree depending on where it is used.

That is the bridge the Specification Pattern relies on: one domain rule can be expressed once as an expression tree, then used both for query translation and for in-memory validation after compilation.

value.caption: Same lambda, different meaning depending on the target type

body.value:
A first improvement is to store business rules as plain C# expressions and reuse them. That removes duplication and supports both querying and validation.

For querying, the expression is passed directly into the ORM. For validation, the expression is compiled into a delegate and executed in memory.

This is a real improvement, but it still exposes too much low-level detail to the client code.

value.caption: Reusable rules as plain expressions

value.title: Why plain expressions are not enough
value.text: Plain expressions reduce duplication, but the client still has to know when to compile them, how to combine them, and how to pass them around. That means the abstraction is still too low-level.

value.title: Why generic specifications are also weak

body.value:
Another tempting design is a generic wrapper such as GenericSpecification<T> that accepts any expression through the constructor. That looks better because it adds helper methods like IsSatisfiedBy, but it still does not truly encapsulate domain knowledge.

The business rule still comes from the client. The specification object is just a transport wrapper around whatever the caller decided to pass in. In other words, the rule is still externally defined instead of being modeled explicitly in the domain.

A proper specification should contain the business rule, not merely receive it.

value.caption: A generic specification is usually only a thin wrapper

value.title: The better design: strongly typed specifications

body.value:
The stronger approach is to create a base specification type and then derive concrete, strongly typed specifications from it. Each concrete class represents a precise piece of business knowledge with a clear name and meaning.

For example:

  • MovieForKidsSpecification
  • AvailableOnCDSpecification

Now the caller no longer builds the rule. The caller simply chooses the rule object it needs.

value.caption: Strongly typed specifications contain business meaning
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Base[Specification<T>] --> Kids[MovieForKidsSpecification]
Base --> CD[AvailableOnCDSpecification]
Kids --> QueryUse[Used in database queries]
Kids --> ValidationUse[Used in in-memory validation]
CD --> QueryUse2[Used in database queries]
CD --> ValidationUse2[Used in in-memory validation]

value.caption: A clean base class for strongly typed specifications
Concrete specifications with explicit business meaning

value.title: Using the same specification for validation and querying

body.value:
This is where the pattern becomes elegant. The same MovieForKidsSpecification can be used in two different places without duplicating the rule:

  • In a repository, by passing spec.ToExpression() into LINQ-to-database.
  • In application or domain logic, by calling spec.IsSatisfiedBy(movie).

The business rule is declared once and reused safely.

value.caption: One rule, two execution contexts

body.value:
Simple specifications are useful, but the real power comes from combining them. In business systems, requirements are often made of smaller reusable rules.

The most common combination types are:

  • And — both rules must be satisfied
  • Or — either rule may be satisfied
  • Not — invert a rule

For example, you may want to search for movies that are both suitable for kids and available on CD, or movies available on CD but not suitable for children.

value.caption: Composing larger rules from smaller ones
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Kids[MovieForKidsSpecification] --> AndSpec[And]
CD[AvailableOnCDSpecification] --> AndSpec
AndSpec --> Result1[Kids movie available on CD]
CD --> AndSpec2[And]
NotKids[Not(MovieForKidsSpecification)] --> AndSpec2
AndSpec2 --> Result2[Movie on CD but not for kids]

value.caption: Base class helpers for composition
A simple And specification built on expression trees

body.value: The exact implementation of composition varies by project, and some teams prefer additional expression visitors to avoid provider limitations. But the key design idea remains the same: composition belongs inside the specification abstraction, not in client code.

When building dynamic queries, it is useful to have a neutral starting point: a specification that always returns true. This is often called an identity specification.

It acts like a null object. You start with All, then add criteria only when the user selects them. This makes dynamic filtering much cleaner.

value.caption: Identity specification for dynamic search

value.title: Why the identity specification is useful
value.text: It lets you build up dynamic search conditions step by step without null checks and without special branching for the first condition.

body.value:
A few practical rules make this pattern much easier to maintain.

  • Use strongly typed specifications instead of generic wrappers.
  • Make them as specific as possible. Prefer MovieForKidsSpecification over a vague, over-parameterized rule unless variation is truly necessary.
  • Keep them immutable. If a rule changes, create a new specification instance instead of mutating an old one.
  • Avoid unnecessary interfaces when the base class already provides the needed abstraction.

The naming of specifications matters. Good names communicate domain intent and reduce cognitive load for the rest of the team.

value.title: A common anti-pattern: returning IQueryable from repositories

body.value:
One of the strongest warnings around this topic is to avoid returning IQueryable from public repository methods in medium or large codebases.

Returning IQueryable looks flexible because callers can append filters however they like. But that flexibility is misleading. The method signature effectively promises that any valid LINQ expression can be used, while the underlying ORM can translate only a subset of them. That is a leaky abstraction.

This is often framed as a violation of the Liskov Substitution Principle: the public API appears to support more than the implementation can actually fulfill.

value.caption: Avoid exposing IQueryable from public methods

value.title: Repository return types
value.text: If you do not intentionally need deferred execution outside the repository, prefer materialized results such as IReadOnlyList over IQueryable. IQueryable exposes persistence details too far up the stack.

body.value:
Not every query condition deserves its own specification. If a condition is only needed in one place, such as a repository-only filter by minimum rating or paging parameters, it is usually fine to keep that logic directly in the query method.

The Specification Pattern is not an excuse to force every filter into its own class. Use specifications where they remove real duplication and improve modeling. Use ordinary query clauses where they are simpler and sufficient.

value.caption: A specification can coexist with ordinary query filters

body.value:
Specifications are not limited to a single class with primitive fields. They can also include related entities, such as a movie and its director.

For example, a rule like movie directed by a specific person is still a valid specification. In that case, the expression simply navigates through the relationship.

When querying with related entities, remember that your ORM may use lazy loading by default. If the UI or downstream code needs related data after the context is disposed, you may need eager loading or projection to DTOs.

value.caption: A specification involving a related entity

value.title: When not to use the Specification Pattern

body.value:
This pattern is useful, but it is not mandatory for every project.

You can usually skip it when:

  • You only have one relevant use case, such as querying only or validation only.
  • The application is small and unlikely to grow much in complexity.
  • The business rule is so simple and local that introducing more classes would add ceremony without reducing maintenance cost.

The pattern becomes worthwhile when the codebase is large enough, the domain has meaningful business rules, and the same rules must be reused in multiple contexts.

value.caption: A practical decision guide
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Do you reuse the same business rule in multiple places?] -->|No| B[Do not use Specification yet]
A -->|Yes| C[Do you need validation and querying to share the rule?]
C -->|No| D[Regular query or validation code is enough]
C -->|Yes| E[Specification is a strong candidate]
E --> F[Use strongly typed immutable specifications]

value.title: The third use case: creation of new objects

body.value:
The final use case is less common but conceptually important. A specification can describe what a valid candidate should look like, and a system can then search or generate candidate objects that satisfy it.

This is similar to in-memory validation, but the input object does not already exist. Instead, you start from a candidate set and test each candidate against one or more specifications.

In practice, this often becomes a brute-force or search-based problem. The specification itself is still useful, but the harder part is usually generating the candidate set rather than evaluating the rule.

value.title: A complete example in one flow

body.value:
When applied well, the pattern creates a nice pipeline:

  1. Model business rules explicitly as strongly typed specifications.
  2. Reuse them for in-memory validation through IsSatisfiedBy.
  3. Reuse them for efficient database querying through ToExpression().
  4. Compose them with And, Or, and Not when requirements become more sophisticated.

This keeps business rules centralized, readable, and reusable without sacrificing query efficiency.

value.caption: End-to-end flow of a healthy specification-based design
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Rule[Business rule] --> StrongSpec[Strongly typed specification]
StrongSpec --> Query[LINQ to database]
StrongSpec --> Validate[In-memory validation]
StrongSpec --> Compose[AND / OR / NOT composition]
Compose --> ComplexRules[More advanced business rules]
Query --> EfficientSql[Efficient SQL]
Validate --> ConsistentBehavior[Consistent behavior]

body.value:
The Specification Pattern is not about wrapping predicates in fancy classes. It is about making business rules first-class citizens in your codebase.

When implemented with strongly typed specifications, it helps you avoid duplicating domain knowledge, preserve encapsulation, and keep database access efficient. When combined thoughtfully, it also gives you a clear and expressive language for modeling business requirements.

If your system needs the same rule for both validation and querying, this pattern is often one of the cleanest ways to keep the design under control.

title: C# State Pattern Explained: Cleaner State Transitions Without Boolean Spaghetti
excerpt: Learn how the State pattern works in C#, why it reduces conditional complexity, and how to model state transitions cleanly using a booking workflow with New, Pending, Booked, and Closed states.

body.value:
The State pattern is designed for objects whose behavior changes based on their internal state. It becomes especially valuable when a class starts accumulating if, else, or switch branches just to decide what should happen in each situation.

A common symptom is a method that started simple but slowly turned into a bundle of flags such as isNew, isPending, isBooked, and several condition chains that all need to stay synchronized. The code still works for a while, but it becomes harder to read, harder to test, and risky to extend.

The State pattern solves that by moving state-specific behavior into separate classes. The main object no longer needs to know every detail about every possible state. It delegates work to the object that represents its current state.

value.caption: Core idea of the State pattern
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Context[BookingContext] --> StateInterface[BookingState]
StateInterface --> NewState[NewState]
StateInterface --> PendingState[PendingState]
StateInterface --> BookedState[BookedState]
StateInterface --> ClosedState[ClosedState]

value.title: Why naive state handling becomes painful

body.value:
Imagine a booking workflow. A booking can be new, pending, booked, or closed. The user can submit details, cancel, or wait until the event date passes.

A naive implementation often starts with a single class and a few booleans. That may feel natural at first, but every new state introduces more checks, more transitions, and more places where a flag has to be set correctly. Eventually, methods stop describing business behavior and start describing defensive bookkeeping.

This is the real cost: every new requirement tends to force changes in code that was already working. Adding a new state does not stay local. It spills into several methods and increases the chance of subtle bugs.

value.caption: How conditional complexity grows in a naive design
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Start[Booking class] --> Flags[Boolean flags: isNew / isPending / isBooked]
Flags --> Cancel[Cancel method]
Flags --> Submit[SubmitDetails method]
Flags --> DatePassed[DatePassed method]
Cancel --> IfElse1[if / else if / else]
Submit --> IfElse2[if / else if / else]
DatePassed --> IfElse3[if / else if / else]
IfElse1 --> Coupling[Behavior and state tracking mixed together]
IfElse2 --> Coupling
IfElse3 --> Coupling

value.title: A practical warning sign
value.text: When a class needs multiple booleans just to describe what mode it is in, and several methods keep checking those booleans, the State pattern is often worth considering.

body.value:
The State pattern is usually described through three roles.

  • Context: the main object whose behavior changes over time. In this example, that is the booking itself.
  • State: an abstract base class or interface that defines the operations the context can delegate.
  • Concrete States: focused implementations such as NewState, PendingState, BookedState, and ClosedState.

The context stores a reference to the current state and forwards work to it. Each concrete state decides how that action should behave and whether the context should transition to another state.

value.caption: Minimal abstract state for the booking workflow

value.title: The booking workflow used to explain the pattern

body.value:
A simple event booking system is a strong teaching example because the states are easy to identify and the transitions are clear.

A booking starts in the new state. If the user submits details, it moves to pending. If processing succeeds, it becomes booked. If processing fails, it returns to new. If the user cancels or the event date passes in certain states, it ends up closed.

That is exactly the kind of workflow where the State pattern shines: a single object, a limited set of legitimate states, and behavior that depends strongly on which state is active right now.

value.caption: Booking state transitions
value.diagram:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Pending: SubmitDetails
    New --> Closed: Cancel
    New --> Closed: DatePassed
    Pending --> Booked: ProcessingSuccess
    Pending --> New: ProcessingFailed
    Pending --> Closed: Cancel
    Booked --> Closed: Cancel
    Booked --> Closed: DatePassed

value.title: The context owns the current state

body.value:
The context is the object the rest of the application talks to. In the course example, the UI does not work directly with NewState or PendingState. It works with BookingContext.

BookingContext stores business data such as attendee name, ticket count, booking ID, and a reference to the current state. When the UI calls methods like SubmitDetails, Cancel, or DatePassed, the context simply forwards that call to the current state object.

This is the heart of the pattern. The context remains stable, while the current state determines the actual behavior.

value.caption: Booking context delegating work to the current state

value.title: Entering a state is part of the design

body.value:
One especially useful idea in this implementation is the EnterState method. Instead of only switching the current state reference, the context also gives the new state a chance to run setup logic as soon as it becomes active.

That makes the transition explicit and gives each state a place to perform entry behavior. For a booking, entering the new state can assign a booking ID, update the UI, and show the data entry form. Entering the booked state can update the UI and show a confirmation message.

This keeps setup code close to the state that owns it.

value.caption: What happens during a transition
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    participant UI
    participant Context as BookingContext
    participant State as Current BookingState

    UI->>Context: SubmitDetails(...)
    Context->>State: EnterDetails(context, attendee, ticketCount)
    State->>Context: TransitionToState(new PendingState())
    Context->>State: set currentState
    Context->>State: EnterState(context)

body.value:
Each concrete state implements the same interface, but only the behavior that makes sense for that state. For example, NewState accepts user input and can transition either to PendingState or ClosedState.

Notice what it does not do: it does not check whether the booking is pending, booked, or already closed. That is unnecessary because the current object is already NewState. The pattern removes entire categories of defensive conditional logic.

value.caption: Example NewState implementation

value.title: Pending state: where asynchronous work fits naturally

body.value:
The pending state is a good example of why this pattern is so helpful. Once details are submitted, the booking is effectively waiting for an external result. In the demo project, an asynchronous method simulates processing.

That makes PendingState a very natural place to hold the processing workflow, cancellation token, and the logic that reacts to success, failure, or cancellation. None of that needs to clutter the context or the other states.

In other words, the state object becomes the home for behavior that only matters while that state is active.

value.caption: PendingState coordinating processing and transitions

body.value:
In the naive version, a single class had to answer questions like: Is the booking new? Pending? Booked? Closed? Can it be canceled now? Should the date passing matter? What happens after a failed process?

In the State pattern version, those questions disappear from the context. The object is in exactly one state at a time, and the state class itself defines the valid behavior.

This means you do not have to write code that says, in effect, if I am in state A do this, but if I am in state B do something else. The current state object already embodies that decision.

value.caption: Why the current state simplifies logic
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Call[User action] --> Context[BookingContext]
Context --> CurrentState[Current BookingState]
CurrentState --> OnePath[Only behavior for this state runs]
OnePath --> OptionalTransition[Optional transition to another state]

value.title: The biggest architectural gain
value.text: The State pattern does not just move conditionals around. It changes the model so that the active state determines behavior directly, which sharply reduces invalid branching.

body.value:
The most visible benefit is cleaner code, but there are several practical advantages.

  • Lower conditional complexity: fewer long methods with nested branching.
  • Better separation of concerns: each state owns its own behavior.
  • Safer changes: adding or modifying one state is less likely to break another.
  • Improved readability: the workflow is expressed through explicit classes and transitions.
  • Better testability: each state can be tested in isolation.

This pattern is especially useful in workflow-heavy systems such as payments, order processing, approvals, onboarding, subscriptions, document review, and UI flows.

value.title: How to test a state-driven design

body.value:
State-driven systems become easier to test because state behavior is localized. Instead of setting several booleans and invoking one huge method, a test can create the context, move it into a known state, trigger an action, and assert the resulting transition.

That style of testing aligns with the business workflow and tends to produce much more meaningful test names and scenarios.

value.caption: Example unit test for a transition

body.value: In production code you would usually avoid exposing the current state directly unless you have a clean way to inspect it, but the example shows the testing idea clearly: act on a known state and verify the new one.

value.title: Tradeoffs and when not to use it

body.value:
The State pattern is powerful, but it is not free.

  • It introduces more classes than a single naive implementation.
  • It takes more initial design work.
  • It can feel heavy for tiny workflows with only one or two trivial branches.

If the object barely changes behavior and the workflow is unlikely to grow, a simpler design may be enough. But once state handling starts spreading across multiple methods and flags, the State pattern usually pays for itself quickly.

value.title: Good fit vs overkill
value.text: Use the State pattern when states are clear, transitions matter, and behavior genuinely changes by state. Avoid it when the workflow is tiny and unlikely to evolve.

body.value:
The State pattern helps an object change behavior cleanly as its internal state changes. Instead of centralizing all rules in one growing class, it distributes behavior across focused state classes and lets a context delegate to the current one.

In C#, this leads to code that is easier to read, easier to test, easier to extend, and far less dependent on booleans and long conditional chains. The booking example shows the pattern clearly, but the same approach applies to many business workflows where valid actions depend on the current step in a process.

If your class is starting to look like a state machine hidden inside if statements, the State pattern is often the right next move.
